01 / About
ABOUT ME

I’m a mechanical engineering student with a strong interest in automotive engineering, inspired by a childhood spent building with LEGO and a passion for cars. I’m currently working in a research lab focused on thermal systems, where I’m designing a flow boiling system and gaining hands-on experience in engineering design and experimentation.

I enjoy staying active through working out, and in my free time I like playing video games and badminton just for fun. I’m always open to trying new things and pushing myself to learn and grow through new experiences.

Where it all started — Lego Technic & Hot Wheels

Currently

B.S. Mechanical Engineering — University of Illinois Chicago (UIC)

02 / Projects
FEATURED WORK
01 Research · Thermal Systems
FLOW-BOILING TEST MODULE
Tools: SolidWorks · LabVIEW · NI DAQ · Machine Shop

Designed and modeled components for a modular flow-boiling test module and closed-loop thermal system at Suh Lab. Developed a closed-loop schematic integrating a pre-heater, flow meter, thermocouples, and pressure sensors with NI DAQ data logging and LabVIEW monitoring — improving system repeatability and data accuracy.

System Schematic — Draft Design
Full closed-loop system: Pump → Filter → Flowmeter → Pre-Heater → Test Section → Condenser → Reservoir · K/T-Type Thermocouples, Pressure Transducers & DAQ
Test Section — SolidWorks Design
Test Section Assembly
Exploded View
① Acrylic Cover

Transparent top cover allowing visual observation of the boiling process during testing.

② Holder & Screw Holes

Main structural housing that clamps the assembly and routes fluid through precision-machined ports.

③ Copper Block

High-conductivity copper block with micro-fin surface geometry — the primary heat transfer surface where boiling occurs.

④ Ceramic Heater

Electrical heater beneath the copper block providing controlled, uniform heat flux to the test surface.

⑤ Base Holder

Intermediate mounting plate that positions and secures the copper block and heater within the assembly.

⑥ Bottom Brace

Structural base plate providing rigidity to the full assembly and acting as the foundation for bolt clamping.

→

CUSTOM GO-KART CHASSIS

04 College of DuPage · Engineering Final
TENNIS BALL LAUNCHER
Tools: Creo · AutoCAD · Welding

A 6-person team engineering challenge with a strict $100 budget. The result: a spring-loaded launcher with an adjustable pivot, PVC barrel, and a repurposed stop sign pole — designed in CAD, built in a garage, and tested on a field.

⚙ Design & Engineering
  • Designed a 3 ft × 3 ft frame in Creo and AutoCAD, reducing material usage by 93%.
  • Front plate features spring connection points; adjustable pivot allows launch angle tuning.
  • All components checked against design and safety specs before fabrication.
🔨 Build & Fabrication
  • Compression-spring and hollow PVC barrel mechanism for consistent, repeatable launches — improving shot consistency by 57%.
  • Repurposed a stop sign pole as vertical support structure at near-zero cost.
  • Welded frame on a reclaimed wood pallet base for stability and portability.
🎯 Results
  • Extended effective launch range by 83% through barrel alignment and pivot angle optimisation.
  • Delivered fully functional launcher under $100 with documented CAD models and parts list.
  • Demonstrated resourceful material sourcing paired with solid CAD planning.
Build Photos & Design Sketches
Physical Build
Design Diagram
Design Sketch
→
05 ME-347 · Product Design
FIDGET KEYBOARD CUBE
Tools: SolidWorks · FDM 3D Printing · MakerSpace

Designed a pocket-sized 5-key mini keyboard fidget toy that transforms into a cube — fully 3D-printable with zero post-assembly. Modeled custom spring mechanisms replicating the tactile feel of keyboard switches, and integrated 8 print-in-place hinges so the keyboard folds seamlessly into cube form.

✎ Brainstorming & Early Sketches
Keyboard Layout Sketch
Spring Mechanism Sketch
Cube / Base Sketch
⚙ Prototype Iterations
1st Print — Too Small
1st Print — Springs Failed
2nd Print — Too Loose
① 1st Prototype — Black

Dimensioned from real mechanical keyboard keys. Turned out too small — spring features completely failed to print at that scale, requiring a full redesign and scale-up.

② 2nd Prototype — Orange

After scaling up, springs printed successfully. But hinge and connection tolerances were too loose — keys wouldn’t stay engaged or fold crisply. Required full tolerance refinement.

✎ Reflection
  • Tolerancing was the hardest challenge across every iteration.
  • Final design submitted to MakerSpace well ahead of deadline for print time.
  • Future: more flexible spring geometry and a more compact cube form factor.
⛤ Final Design — Individual Components
Hinge Design
Spring Design
Keycap Design
Base Design
🎯 Final Assembly & CAD
